infobox.value: Without proper [rendering support](/wiki/Help:IPA#Rendering_issues "Help:IPA"), you may see [question marks, boxes, or other symbols](/wiki/Specials_(Unicode_block)#Replacement_character "Specials (Unicode block)") instead of [Unicode](/wiki/Unicode "Unicode") characters. For an introductory guide on IPA symbols, see [Help:IPA](/wiki/Help:IPA "Help:IPA"). | |

([endonym](/wiki/Endonym "Endonym"): *português* or *língua portuguesa*) is a [Western Romance language](/wiki/Western_Romance_language "Western Romance language") of the [Indo-European language family](/wiki/Indo-European_language_family "Indo-European language family") originating from the [Iberian Peninsula](/wiki/Iberian_Peninsula "Iberian Peninsula") of [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"). It is spoken chiefly in Portugal, in Brazil, and in several African countries, as well as by immigrants in North America, Europe, and South America. With approximately 267 million speakers, it is listed as the [fifth-most spoken native language](/wiki/List_of_languages_by_number_of_native_speakers "List of languages by number of native speakers").

**Portuguese** ([endonym](/wiki/Endonym "Endonym"): *português* or *língua portuguesa*) is a [Western Romance language](/wiki/Western_Romance_language "Western Romance language") of the [Indo-European language family](/wiki/Indo-European_language_family "Indo-European language family") originating from the [Iberian Peninsula](/wiki/Iberian_Peninsula "Iberian Peninsula") of [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"). It is spoken chiefly in Portugal, in Brazil, and in several African countries, as well as by immigrants in North America, Europe, and South America. With approximately 267 million speakers, it is listed as the [fifth-most spoken native language](/wiki/List_of_languages_by_number_of_native_speakers "List of languages by number of native speakers").

Portuguese-speaking people or nations are known as [Lusophone](/wiki/Lusophone "Lusophone") (*lusófono*). As a result of expansion during colonial times, a cultural presence of Portuguese speakers is also found around the world. Portuguese is part of the [Ibero-Romance group](/wiki/Iberian_Romance_languages "Iberian Romance languages"), which evolved from several dialects of [Vulgar Latin](/wiki/Vulgar_Latin "Vulgar Latin") in the medieval [Kingdom of Galicia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Galicia "Kingdom of Galicia") and the [County of Portugal](/wiki/County_of_Portugal "County of Portugal"), and has retained some [Celtic](/wiki/Gallaecian_language "Gallaecian language") phonology.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Portuguese language structure reflects both its Latin roots and centuries of outside influences. These are seen in [phonology](/wiki/Portuguese_phonology "Portuguese phonology"), [orthography](/wiki/Portuguese_orthography "Portuguese orthography"), [grammar](/wiki/Portuguese_grammar "Portuguese grammar"), and [vocabulary](/wiki/Portuguese_vocabulary "Portuguese vocabulary"). Phonologically, Portuguese has a rich system of [nasal vowels](/wiki/Nasal_vowels "Nasal vowels"), complex consonant variations, and different types of [guttural R](/wiki/Guttural_R "Guttural R") - as well as other sounds in its European and Brazilian varieties. Its spelling, based similarly to English on the [Latin alphabet](/wiki/Latin_alphabet "Latin alphabet"), is largely [phonemic](/wiki/Phonemic_spelling "Phonemic spelling") but is influenced by [etymology](/wiki/Etymology "Etymology") and tradition. [Recent spelling reforms](/wiki/Portuguese_spelling_reform_of_1990 "Portuguese spelling reform of 1990") have attempted to create a unified spelling for the Portuguese language across [all countries that use it](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_territories_where_Portuguese_is_an_official_language "List of countries and territories where Portuguese is an official language"). Portuguese grammar retains many [Latin verb forms](/wiki/Latin_verbs "Latin verbs"), and possesses several unique features (such as the [future subjunctive](/wiki/Portuguese_conjugation "Portuguese conjugation") and the [personal infinitive](/wiki/Portuguese_grammar#Infinitive_form "Portuguese grammar")). Its vocabulary is derived largely from Latin, but also includes [numerous loanwords](/w/index.php?title=List_of_loanwords_in_Portuguese&action=edit&redlink=1 "List of loanwords in Portuguese (page does not exist)")[[pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Empr%C3%A9stimo_(lingu%C3%ADstica)#Exemplos_de_empréstimos "pt:Empréstimo (linguística)")] from Celtic, Germanic, Arabic, African, Amerindian, and Asian languages, resulting from historical contact based on wars, trade, and [colonization](/wiki/Portuguese_colonization "Portuguese colonization").

There is significant [variation](/wiki/Variation_(linguistics) "Variation (linguistics)") in the dialects of Portuguese worldwide, with two primary [standardized varieties](/wiki/Standard_language "Standard language"): [European Portuguese](/wiki/European_Portuguese "European Portuguese") and [Brazilian Portuguese](/wiki/Brazilian_Portuguese "Brazilian Portuguese"), each one having numerous [regional accents](/wiki/Regional_accent "Regional accent") and [subdialects](/wiki/Subdialect "Subdialect"). African and Asian varieties generally follow the European written standard, though these will often display different [phonological](/wiki/Phonological "Phonological"), [lexical](/wiki/Lexical_item "Lexical item"), and sometimes even [syntactic features](/wiki/Syntax "Syntax"). While there is broad [mutual intelligibility](/wiki/Mutual_intelligibility "Mutual intelligibility") among the assorted lusophone population, variation may be observed in speech patterns, vocabulary, or grammar.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_language&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of the Portuguese language](/wiki/History_of_the_Portuguese_language "History of the Portuguese language")

See also: [Will of Afonso II of Portugal](/wiki/Will_of_Afonso_II_of_Portugal "Will of Afonso II of Portugal")

### Origins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_language&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Origins")]

When the [Romans](/wiki/Roman_people "Roman people") arrived in the [Iberian Peninsula](/wiki/Iberian_Peninsula "Iberian Peninsula") in 216 BC, they brought with them the [Latin language](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"), from which all [Romance languages](/wiki/Romance_languages "Romance languages") are descended. The language was spread by Roman commoners, merchants, and soldiers, who built Roman cities mostly near the settlements of previous [Celtic](/wiki/Celts "Celts") civilizations established long before the Roman arrivals. For that reason, the language has kept a relevant substratum of much older, [Atlantic European](/wiki/Atlantic_Europe "Atlantic Europe") [Megalithic Culture](/wiki/Megalithic_Culture "Megalithic Culture")[[9]](#cite_note-9) and [Celtic culture](/wiki/Celts "Celts"),[[10]](#cite_note-Gramatica_2007-10) part of the [Hispano-Celtic group](/wiki/Hispano-Celtic_languages "Hispano-Celtic languages") of ancient languages.[[11]](#cite_note-Colera_2007_p.750_quote-11) In Latin, the Portuguese language is known as *lusitana* or *(latina) lusitanica*, after the [Lusitanians](/wiki/Lusitanians "Lusitanians"), a pre-Celtic tribe that lived in the territory of present-day Portugal and Spain that adopted the Latin language as Roman settlers moved in. This is also the origin of the *luso-* prefix, seen in terms like "[Lusophone](/wiki/Lusophone "Lusophone")".

### Germanic Period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_language&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Germanic Period")]

Between AD 409 and AD 711, as the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") collapsed in [Western Europe](/wiki/Western_Europe "Western Europe"), the Iberian Peninsula was conquered by [Germanic peoples](/wiki/Germanic_peoples "Germanic peoples") of the [Migration Period](/wiki/Migration_Period "Migration Period"). The occupiers, most notably [Suebi](/wiki/Suebi "Suebi")[[12]](#cite_note-cornell-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) but also [Visigoths](/wiki/Visigoths "Visigoths") and [Buri](/wiki/Buri_tribe "Buri tribe")[[14]](#cite_note-14) originally spoke [Germanic languages](/wiki/Germanic_languages "Germanic languages"). Quickly, they adopted late Roman culture and the [Vulgar Latin](/wiki/Vulgar_Latin "Vulgar Latin") dialects of the peninsula and over the next 300 years totally integrated into the local population. Some Germanic words from that period are part of the Portuguese lexicon, together with place names, surnames, and first names.

### After the Arab Conquest

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_language&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: After the Arab Conquest")]

With the [Umayyad conquest](/wiki/Umayyad_conquest_of_Hispania "Umayyad conquest of Hispania") beginning in 711, [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic") became the administrative and common language in the conquered regions, but most of the [remaining Christian population](/wiki/Mozarabs "Mozarabs") continued to speak a form of [Ibero-Romance](/wiki/Ibero-Romance "Ibero-Romance") called [Mozarabic](/wiki/Andalusi_Romance "Andalusi Romance") which introduced a few hundred words from Arabic, [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language"), [Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_language "Turkish language"), and [Berber](/wiki/Berber_languages "Berber languages").[[15]](#cite_note-15) Like other Neo-Latin and European languages, Portuguese has adopted a significant number of [loanwords](/wiki/Loanword "Loanword") from [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language"),[[16]](#cite_note-16) mainly in technical and scientific terminology. These borrowings occurred via Latin, and later during the Middle Ages and the Renaissance.

Portuguese evolved from the medieval language spoken in the northwestern medieval [Kingdom of Galicia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Galicia "Kingdom of Galicia"), which the [County of Portugal](/wiki/County_of_Portugal "County of Portugal") once formed part of. This variety has been retrospectively named [Galician–Portuguese](/wiki/Galician%E2%80%93Portuguese "Galician–Portuguese"), Old Portuguese, or Old Galician by linguists.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Idioma_galaicoportugu%C3%A9s.png/250px-Idioma_galaicoportugu%C3%A9s.png)](/wiki/File:Idioma_galaicoportugu%C3%A9s.png)

Spoken area of Galician–Portuguese (also known as Old Portuguese or Medieval Galician) in the kingdoms of Galicia and León around the 10th century, before the separation of [Galician](/wiki/Galician_language "Galician language") and Portuguese

It is in Latin administrative documents of the 9th century that written Galician–Portuguese words and phrases are first recorded. This phase is known as Proto-Portuguese, which lasted from the 9th century until the 12th-century independence of the [County of Portugal](/wiki/County_of_Portugal "County of Portugal") from the [Kingdom of León](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Le%C3%B3n "Kingdom of León"), which had by then assumed reign over [Galicia](/wiki/Galicia_(Spain) "Galicia (Spain)").

In the first part of the Galician–Portuguese period (from the 12th to the 14th century), the language was increasingly used for documents and other written forms. For some time, it was the language of preference for [lyric poetry](/wiki/Lyric_poetry "Lyric poetry") in Christian [Hispania](/wiki/Hispania "Hispania"), much as [Occitan](/wiki/Occitan_language "Occitan language") was the language of the poetry of the [troubadours](/wiki/Troubadours "Troubadours") in France. The Occitan digraphs *lh* and *nh*, used in its classical orthography, were adopted by the [orthography of Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_alphabet#Basic_digraphs "Portuguese alphabet"), presumably by [Gerald of Braga](/wiki/Gerald_of_Braga "Gerald of Braga"),[[18]](#cite_note-18) a monk from [Moissac](/wiki/Moissac "Moissac"), who became bishop of [Braga](/wiki/Braga "Braga") in Portugal in 1047, playing a major role in modernizing written Portuguese using classical Occitan norms.[[19]](#cite_note-19) Portugal became an independent kingdom in 1139, under King [Afonso I of Portugal](/wiki/Afonso_I_of_Portugal "Afonso I of Portugal"). In 1290, King [Denis of Portugal](/wiki/Denis_of_Portugal "Denis of Portugal") created the first Portuguese university in Lisbon (the *Estudos Gerais*, which later moved to [Coimbra](/wiki/University_of_Coimbra "University of Coimbra")) and decreed for Portuguese, then simply called the "common language", to be known as the Portuguese language and used officially.

In the second period of Old Portuguese, in the 15th and 16th centuries, with the [Portuguese discoveries](/wiki/Age_of_Discovery "Age of Discovery"), the language was taken to many regions of Africa, Asia, and the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas"). By the mid-16th century, Portuguese had become a *[lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca")* in Asia and Africa, used not only for colonial administration and trade but also for communication between local officials and Europeans of all nationalities. The Portuguese expanded across South America, across Africa to the Pacific Ocean, taking their language with them.

Its spread was helped by mixed marriages between Portuguese and local people and by its association with [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") [missionary](/wiki/Missionary "Missionary") efforts, which led to the formation of [creole languages](/wiki/Creole_language "Creole language") such as that called [Kristang](/wiki/Kristang_language "Kristang language") in many parts of Asia (from the word *cristão*, "Christian"). The language continued to be popular in parts of Asia until the 19th century. Some Portuguese-speaking Christian communities in [India](/wiki/India "India"), [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka"), [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"), and [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia") preserved their language even after they were isolated from Portugal.

The end of the Old Portuguese period was marked by the publication of the *Cancioneiro Geral* by [Garcia de Resende](/wiki/Garcia_de_Resende "Garcia de Resende"), in 1516. The early times of Modern Portuguese, which spans the period from the 16th century to the present day, were characterized by an increase in the number of learned words borrowed from [Classical Latin](/wiki/Classical_Latin "Classical Latin") and [Classical Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") because of the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") (learned words borrowed from Latin also came from [Renaissance Latin](/wiki/Renaissance_Latin "Renaissance Latin"), the form of Latin during that time), which greatly enriched the lexicon. Most literate Portuguese speakers were also literate in Latin; and thus they easily adopted Latin words into their writing, and eventually speech, in Portuguese.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

Spanish author [Miguel de Cervantes](/wiki/Miguel_de_Cervantes "Miguel de Cervantes") once called Portuguese "the sweet and gracious language", while the Brazilian poet [Olavo Bilac](/wiki/Olavo_Bilac "Olavo Bilac") described it as *a última flor do Lácio, inculta e bela* ("the last flower of [Latium](/wiki/Latium "Latium"), naïve and beautiful").[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25) Portuguese is also termed "the language of Camões", after [Luís Vaz de Camões](/wiki/Lu%C3%ADs_Vaz_de_Cam%C3%B5es "Luís Vaz de Camões"), one of the greatest literary figures in the Portuguese language and author of the Portuguese [epic poem](/wiki/Epic_poem "Epic poem") *[The Lusiads](/wiki/The_Lusiads "The Lusiads")*.[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)

In March 2006, the [Museum of the Portuguese Language](/wiki/Museum_of_the_Portuguese_Language "Museum of the Portuguese Language"), an interactive museum about the Portuguese language, was founded in [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo "São Paulo"), Brazil, the city with the greatest number of Portuguese language speakers in the world.[[29]](#cite_note-NOVA-29) The museum is the first of its kind in the world.[[29]](#cite_note-NOVA-29) In 2015 the museum was partially destroyed in a fire,[[30]](#cite_note-30) but restored and reopened in 2020.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

## Geographic distribution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_language&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Geographic distribution")]

Main articles: [List of countries and territories where Portuguese is an official language](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_territories_where_Portuguese_is_an_official_language "List of countries and territories where Portuguese is an official language") and [Geographical distribution of Portuguese speakers](/wiki/Geographical_distribution_of_Portuguese_speakers "Geographical distribution of Portuguese speakers")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Portuguese_Language_Map_-_Darker_Blue_-_Mother_Language_of_More_than_50%25.png/250px-Portuguese_Language_Map_-_Darker_Blue_-_Mother_Language_of_More_than_50%25.png)](/wiki/File:Portuguese_Language_Map_-_Darker_Blue_-_Mother_Language_of_More_than_50%25.png)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Portuguese_-_Native_Language_Gradient_2.svg/250px-Portuguese_-_Native_Language_Gradient_2.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Portuguese_-_Native_Language_Gradient_2.svg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/Multilingual_Emergency_Assembly_Area_Sign_in_Oizumi.JPG/250px-Multilingual_Emergency_Assembly_Area_Sign_in_Oizumi.JPG)](/wiki/File:Multilingual_Emergency_Assembly_Area_Sign_in_Oizumi.JPG)

Sign in Japanese, Portuguese, and English in [Oizumi](/wiki/Oizumi "Oizumi"), Japan, which has a large [Lusophone](/wiki/Lusophone "Lusophone") community due to the return immigration of [Japanese Brazilians](/wiki/Japanese_Brazilians "Japanese Brazilians")[[32]](#cite_note-32)

Portuguese is spoken by approximately 200 million people in South America, 30 million in Africa, 15 million in Europe, 5 million in North America and 0.33 million in Asia and Oceania. It is the native language of the vast majority of the people in Portugal,[[33]](#cite_note-Special_Eurobarometer_243-33) Brazil[[34]](#cite_note-34) and São Tomé and Príncipe (95%).[[35]](#cite_note-35) Around 45% of the population of urban Angola speaks Portuguese natively,[[36]](#cite_note-censoAO-36) with approximately 85% fluent; these rates are lower in the countryside.[[37]](#cite_note-37) Just over 50% (and rapidly increasing) of the population of [Mozambique](/wiki/Mozambique "Mozambique") are native speakers of Portuguese, and 70% are fluent, according to the 2007 census.[[38]](#cite_note-38) Portuguese is also spoken natively by 30% of the population in Guinea-Bissau, and a Portuguese-based creole is understood by all.[[39]](#cite_note-39) Almost 50% of the East Timorese are fluent in Portuguese. No data is available for Cape Verde, but almost all the population is bilingual, and the monolingual population speaks the Portuguese-based [Cape Verdean Creole](/wiki/Cape_Verdean_Creole "Cape Verdean Creole"). Portuguese is mentioned in the [Constitution of South Africa](/wiki/Constitution_of_South_Africa "Constitution of South Africa") as one of the languages spoken by communities within the country for which the [Pan South African Language Board](/wiki/Pan_South_African_Language_Board "Pan South African Language Board") was charged with promoting and ensuring respect.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

There are also significant Portuguese-speaking immigrant communities in many territories including [Andorra](/wiki/Andorra "Andorra") (17.1%),[[41]](#cite_note-41) [Bermuda](/wiki/Bermuda "Bermuda"),[[42]](#cite_note-42) [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada") (400,275 people in the 2006 census),[[43]](#cite_note-43) [France](/wiki/France "France") (1,625,000 people),[[44]](#cite_note-44) [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") (400,000 people),[[45]](#cite_note-45) [Jersey](/wiki/Jersey "Jersey"),[[46]](#cite_note-46) [Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg "Luxembourg") (about 25% of the population as of 2021), [Namibia](/wiki/Namibia "Namibia") (about 4–5% of the population, mainly refugees from Angola in the north of the country),[[47]](#cite_note-www.namibian.com.na-47) [Paraguay](/wiki/Paraguay "Paraguay") (10.7% or 636,000 people),[[48]](#cite_note-48) [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland") (550,000 in 2019, learning + mother tongue),[[49]](#cite_note-49) [Venezuela](/wiki/Venezuela "Venezuela") (554,000),[[50]](#cite_note-50) and the United States (0.35% of the population or 1,228,126 speakers according to the 2007 [American Community Survey](/wiki/American_Community_Survey "American Community Survey")).[[51]](#cite_note-51)

In some parts of former [Portuguese India](/wiki/Portuguese_India "Portuguese India"), namely [Goa](/wiki/Goa "Goa")[[52]](#cite_note-52) and [Daman and Diu](/wiki/Daman_and_Diu "Daman and Diu"),[[53]](#cite_note-53) the language is still spoken by about 10,000 people. In 2014, an estimated 1,500 students were learning Portuguese in Goa.[[54]](#cite_note-54) Approximately 2% of the people of Macau, China are fluent speakers of Portuguese. Additionally, the language is being very actively studied in the Chinese school system right up to the doctorate level. The [Kristang people](/wiki/Kristang_people "Kristang people") in [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia") speak [Kristang](/wiki/Kristang_language "Kristang language"), a Portuguese-Malay creole; however, the Portuguese language itself is not widely spoken in the country.

### Official status

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_language&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Official status")]

Main article: [List of countries and territories where Portuguese is an official language](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_territories_where_Portuguese_is_an_official_language "List of countries and territories where Portuguese is an official language")

The [Community of Portuguese Language Countries](/wiki/Community_of_Portuguese_Language_Countries "Community of Portuguese Language Countries")[[55]](#cite_note-CPLP-55)
(in Portuguese *Comunidade dos Países de Língua Portuguesa*, with the Portuguese acronym CPLP) consists of the nine independent countries that have Portuguese as an [official language](/wiki/Official_language "Official language"): [Angola](/wiki/Angola "Angola"), [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil"), [Cape Verde](/wiki/Cape_Verde "Cape Verde"), [East Timor](/wiki/East_Timor "East Timor"), [Equatorial Guinea](/wiki/Equatorial_Guinea "Equatorial Guinea"), [Guinea-Bissau](/wiki/Guinea-Bissau "Guinea-Bissau"), [Mozambique](/wiki/Mozambique "Mozambique"), [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal") and [São Tomé and Príncipe](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Tom%C3%A9_and_Pr%C3%ADncipe "São Tomé and Príncipe").[[55]](#cite_note-CPLP-55)

[Equatorial Guinea](/wiki/Equatorial_Guinea "Equatorial Guinea") made a formal application for full membership to the CPLP in June 2010, a status given only to states with Portuguese as an official language.[[56]](#cite_note-56) Portuguese became its third official language (besides [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language") and [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"))[[57]](#cite_note-57) in 2011, and in July 2014, the country was accepted as a member of the CPLP.[[58]](#cite_note-58)

Portuguese is also one of the official languages of the Special Administrative Region of the People's Republic of China of Macau (alongside [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language")) and of several international organizations, including [Mercosul](/wiki/Mercosul "Mercosul"),[[59]](#cite_note-59) the [Organization of Ibero-American States](/wiki/Organization_of_Ibero-American_States "Organization of Ibero-American States"),[[60]](#cite_note-60) the [Union of South American Nations](/wiki/Union_of_South_American_Nations "Union of South American Nations"),[[61]](#cite_note-61) the [Organization of American States](/wiki/Organization_of_American_States "Organization of American States"),[[62]](#cite_note-62) the [African Union](/wiki/African_Union "African Union"),[[63]](#cite_note-africa-union.org-63) the [Economic Community of West African States](/wiki/Economic_Community_of_West_African_States "Economic Community of West African States"),[[63]](#cite_note-africa-union.org-63) the [Southern African Development Community](/wiki/Southern_African_Development_Community "Southern African Development Community")[[63]](#cite_note-africa-union.org-63) and the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union").[[64]](#cite_note-64)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_language&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Lusophone countries")]

According to *[The World Factbook](/wiki/The_World_Factbook "The World Factbook")*'s country population estimates for 2018, the population of each of the ten jurisdictions is as follows (by descending order):

[Macau](/wiki/Macau "Macau") is one of the two autonomous [Special Administrative Regions of the People's Republic of China](/wiki/Special_Administrative_Region_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Special Administrative Region of the People's Republic of China") (the other being [Anglophone](/wiki/English_language "English language") [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong"), a former British colony).
2. [Equatorial Guinea](/wiki/Equatorial_Guinea "Equatorial Guinea") adopted Portuguese as one of its official languages in 2007, being admitted to CPLP in 2014. The use of the Portuguese language in this country is limited, but quickly growing.

The combined population of the entire [Lusophone](/wiki/Lusophone "Lusophone") area was estimated at 300 million in January 2022.[[67]](#cite_note-auto1-67)[[66]](#cite_note-auto-66)[[74]](#cite_note-74) This number does not include the Lusophone [diaspora](/wiki/Diaspora "Diaspora"), estimated at 10 million people (including 4.5 million Portuguese, 3 million Brazilians), although there are no official accurate figures for diasporic Portuguese speakers because a significant portion of these citizens are naturalized citizens born outside of Lusophone territory or are children of immigrants, and may have only a basic command of the language. Additionally, a large part of the diaspora is a part of the already-counted population of the Portuguese-speaking countries and territories, such as the high number of Brazilian and [PALOP](/wiki/Portuguese-speaking_African_countries "Portuguese-speaking African countries") emigrant citizens in Portugal or the high number of Portuguese emigrant citizens in the PALOP and Brazil.

The Portuguese language therefore serves more than 250 million people daily, who have direct or indirect legal, juridical and social contact with it, varying from the only language used in any contact, to only education, contact with local or international administration, commerce and services or the simple sight of road signs, public information and advertising in Portuguese.

### Portuguese as a foreign language

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_language&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Portuguese as a foreign language")]

Portuguese is a mandatory subject in the school curriculum in [Uruguay](/wiki/Uruguay "Uruguay").[[75]](#cite_note-75) Other countries where Portuguese is commonly taught in schools or where it has been introduced as an option include [Venezuela](/wiki/Venezuela "Venezuela"),[[76]](#cite_note-76) [Zambia](/wiki/Zambia "Zambia"),[[77]](#cite_note-77) the [Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Republic_of_the_Congo "Republic of the Congo"),[[78]](#cite_note-estadao-78) [Senegal](/wiki/Senegal "Senegal"),[[78]](#cite_note-estadao-78) [Namibia](/wiki/Namibia "Namibia"),[[47]](#cite_note-www.namibian.com.na-47) [Eswatini](/wiki/Eswatini "Eswatini"),[[78]](#cite_note-estadao-78) [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa"),[[78]](#cite_note-estadao-78) [Ivory Coast](/wiki/Ivory_Coast "Ivory Coast"),[[79]](#cite_note-79) and [Mauritius](/wiki/Mauritius "Mauritius").[[80]](#cite_note-80) In 2017, a project was launched to introduce Portuguese as a school subject in [Zimbabwe](/wiki/Zimbabwe "Zimbabwe").[[81]](#cite_note-81)[[82]](#cite_note-82) Also, according to Portugal's Minister of Foreign Affairs, the language will be part of the school curriculum of a total of 32 countries by 2020.[[83]](#cite_note-83) In such countries, Portuguese is spoken either as a native language by vast majorities due to their Portuguese colonial past or as a *[lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca")* in bordering and multilingual regions, such as on the Brazilian borders of Uruguay and Paraguay and in regions of Angola and Namibia. In many other countries, Portuguese is spoken by majorities as a second language. There remain communities of thousands of Portuguese (or [Creole](/wiki/Creole_language "Creole language")) first language speakers in [Goa](/wiki/Goa "Goa"), [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka"), [Kuala Lumpur](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur "Kuala Lumpur"), [Daman and Diu](/wiki/Daman_and_Diu "Daman and Diu"), and other areas due to [Portuguese colonization](/wiki/Portuguese_Empire "Portuguese Empire"). In [East Timor](/wiki/Timor-Leste "Timor-Leste"), the number of Portuguese speakers is quickly increasing as Portuguese and Brazilian teachers are making great strides in teaching Portuguese in the schools all over the island.[[84]](#cite_note-84) Additionally, there are many large Portuguese-speaking immigrant communities all over the world.

### Future

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_language&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Future")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/MC_%E6%BE%B3%E9%96%80_Macau_%E6%B8%AF%E7%8F%A0%E6%BE%B3%E5%A4%A7%E6%A9%8B_HK-Zhu-Macau_Bridge_port_building_Jan_2019_IX2_63.jpg/250px-MC_%E6%BE%B3%E9%96%80_Macau_%E6%B8%AF%E7%8F%A0%E6%BE%B3%E5%A4%A7%E6%A9%8B_HK-Zhu-Macau_Bridge_port_building_Jan_2019_IX2_63.jpg)](/wiki/File:MC_%E6%BE%B3%E9%96%80_Macau_%E6%B8%AF%E7%8F%A0%E6%BE%B3%E5%A4%A7%E6%A9%8B_HK-Zhu-Macau_Bridge_port_building_Jan_2019_IX2_63.jpg)

Multilingual signage in Chinese, Portuguese and English at the [Hong Kong–Zhuhai–Macau Bridge](/wiki/Hong_Kong%E2%80%93Zhuhai%E2%80%93Macau_Bridge "Hong Kong–Zhuhai–Macau Bridge") port building in [Macau](/wiki/Macau "Macau"). Portuguese is a co-official language in Macau.

According to estimates by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO"), Portuguese is the fastest-growing [European language](/wiki/European_language "European language") after [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") and the language has, according to the newspaper *[The Portugal News](/wiki/The_Portugal_News "The Portugal News")* publishing data given from UNESCO, the highest potential for growth as an international language in [southern Africa](/wiki/Southern_Africa "Southern Africa") and [South America](/wiki/South_America "South America").[[86]](#cite_note-The_Portugal_News-86) Portuguese is a globalized language spoken officially on five continents, and as a second language by millions worldwide.

Since 1991, when Brazil signed into the economic community of [Mercosul](/wiki/Mercosul "Mercosul") with other South American nations, namely [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina"), [Uruguay](/wiki/Uruguay "Uruguay") and [Paraguay](/wiki/Paraguay "Paraguay"), Portuguese is either mandatory, or taught, in the schools of those South American countries.

Although early in the 21st century, after [Macau](/wiki/Macau "Macau") was returned to [China](/wiki/China "China") and immigration of Brazilians of [Japanese descent](/wiki/Japanese_Brazilian "Japanese Brazilian") to [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") slowed down, the use of Portuguese was in decline in [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia"), it is once again becoming a language of opportunity there, mostly because of increased diplomatic and financial ties with economically powerful Portuguese-speaking countries in the world.[[87]](#cite_note-87)[[88]](#cite_note-88)

### Current status and importance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_language&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Current status and importance")]

Portuguese, being a language spread on all continents, has official status in several international organizations. It is one of twenty official languages of the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), an official language of NATO, the [Organization of American States](/wiki/Organization_of_American_States "Organization of American States") (alongside Spanish, French and English), and one of eighteen official languages of the [European Space Agency](/wiki/European_Space_Agency "European Space Agency").

Portuguese is a working language in nonprofit organisations such as the [Red Cross](/wiki/International_Red_Cross_and_Red_Crescent_Movement "International Red Cross and Red Crescent Movement") (alongside English, German, Spanish, French, Arabic and Russian), [Amnesty International](/wiki/Amnesty_International "Amnesty International") (alongside 32 other languages of which English is the most used, followed by Spanish, French, German, and Italian), and [Médecins sans Frontières](/wiki/M%C3%A9decins_sans_Fronti%C3%A8res "Médecins sans Frontières") (used alongside English, Spanish, French and Arabic), in addition to being the official legal language in the [African Court on Human and Peoples' Rights](/wiki/African_Court_on_Human_and_Peoples%27_Rights "African Court on Human and Peoples' Rights"), also in [Community of Portuguese Language Countries](/wiki/Community_of_Portuguese_Language_Countries "Community of Portuguese Language Countries"), an international organization formed essentially by [lusophone countries](/wiki/Lusophone "Lusophone").

## Linguistic demography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_language&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Linguistic demography")]

With approximately 250 million native speakers and 17 million [second language](/wiki/Second_language "Second language") speakers, Portuguese has approximately 267 million total speakers.[[89]](#cite_note-89) It is usually listed as the [fifth-most spoken native language](/wiki/List_of_languages_by_number_of_native_speakers "List of languages by number of native speakers"),[[90]](#cite_note-90) the third-most spoken European language in the world in terms of native speakers[[91]](#cite_note-91) and the second-most spoken [Romance language](/wiki/Romance_languages "Romance languages") in the world, surpassed only by [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"). Being the first most widely spoken language in [South America](/wiki/South_America "South America")[[92]](#cite_note-92)[[93]](#cite_note-93)[[94]](#cite_note-94) and the most-spoken language in the [Southern Hemisphere](/wiki/Southern_Hemisphere "Southern Hemisphere"),[[95]](#cite_note-95)[[96]](#cite_note-96)[[97]](#cite_note-97) it is also the second-most spoken language, after Spanish, in [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America"), one of the 10 most spoken languages in [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa"),[[98]](#cite_note-98) and an official language of the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), [Mercosul](/wiki/Mercosul "Mercosul"), the [Organization of American States](/wiki/Organization_of_American_States#Official_languages "Organization of American States"), the [Economic Community of West African States](/wiki/Economic_Community_of_West_African_States "Economic Community of West African States"), the [African Union](/wiki/African_Union "African Union"), and the [Community of Portuguese Language Countries](/wiki/Community_of_Portuguese_Language_Countries "Community of Portuguese Language Countries"), an international organization made up of all of the world's officially [Lusophone](/wiki/Lusophone "Lusophone") nations. In 1997, a comprehensive academic study ranked Portuguese as one of the 10 most influential languages in the world.[[99]](#cite_note-99)[[100]](#cite_note-100)

## Classification and related languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_language&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Classification and related languages")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/Linguistic_map_Southwestern_Europe-en.gif/500px-Linguistic_map_Southwestern_Europe-en.gif)](/wiki/File:Linguistic_map_Southwestern_Europe-en.gif)

Map showing the historical retreat and expansion of Portuguese ([Galician–Portuguese](/wiki/Galician%E2%80%93Portuguese "Galician–Portuguese")) within the context of its linguistic neighbors between the year 1000 and 2000

 [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Lenguas_y_dialectos_iberorromances.PNG/500px-Lenguas_y_dialectos_iberorromances.PNG)](/wiki/File:Lenguas_y_dialectos_iberorromances.PNG)

Map showing mostly contemporary [West Iberian](/wiki/West_Iberian_languages "West Iberian languages") and [Occitano-Romance](/wiki/Occitano-Romance_languages "Occitano-Romance languages") languages, as well many of their mainland European dialects (areas colored green, [gold](/wiki/Aragonese_language "Aragonese language") or pink/purple represent languages deemed [endangered](/wiki/Endangered_language "Endangered language") by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO"), so this may be outdated in less than a few decades). It shows European Portuguese, [Galician](/wiki/Galician_language "Galician language"), [Eonavian](/wiki/Galician-Asturian "Galician-Asturian"), [Mirandese](/wiki/Mirandese_language "Mirandese language") and the [Fala](/wiki/Fala_language "Fala language") as not only closely related but as [dialect continuum](/wiki/Dialect_continuum "Dialect continuum"), though it excludes dialects spoken in insular Portugal (Azores and Madeira–[Canaries](/wiki/Canarian_Spanish "Canarian Spanish") is not shown either).[*[image reference needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Main articles: [Iberian Romance languages](/wiki/Iberian_Romance_languages "Iberian Romance languages"), [Galician–Portuguese](/wiki/Galician%E2%80%93Portuguese "Galician–Portuguese"), and [Comparison of Spanish and Portuguese](/wiki/Comparison_of_Spanish_and_Portuguese "Comparison of Spanish and Portuguese")

Portuguese belongs to the [West Iberian](/wiki/West_Iberian_languages "West Iberian languages") branch of the [Romance languages](/wiki/Romance_language "Romance language"), and it has special ties with the following members of this group:

* [Galician](/wiki/Galician_language "Galician language") and [Fala](/wiki/Fala_language "Fala language").
* [Mirandese](/wiki/Mirandese_language "Mirandese language"), [Leonese](/wiki/Leonese_language "Leonese language"), [Asturian](/wiki/Asturian_language "Asturian language"), [Extremaduran](/wiki/Extremaduran_language "Extremaduran language") and [Cantabrian](/wiki/Cantabrian_dialect "Cantabrian dialect") ([Astur-Leonese languages](/wiki/Astur-Leonese_languages "Astur-Leonese languages")). Mirandese is the only recognised regional language spoken in Portugal (beside Portuguese, the only official language in Portugal).
* [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language") and [*calão*](/wiki/Cal%C3%B3_language "Caló language") (the Portuguese term for *caló*, language of the Iberian [Romani](/wiki/Romani_people "Romani people")).

Portuguese and other Romance languages (namely [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") and [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language")) share considerable similarities in both vocabulary and grammar. Portuguese speakers will usually need some formal study before attaining strong comprehension in those Romance languages, and vice versa. However, Portuguese and [Galician](/wiki/Galician_language "Galician language") are fully mutually intelligible. Spanish is relatively intelligible for lusophones, owing to their geographic proximity and history as [West Iberian](/wiki/West_Iberian_languages "West Iberian languages") ([Ibero-Romance languages](/wiki/Ibero-Romance_languages "Ibero-Romance languages")), historical contact between speakers and mutual influence, shared [areal features](/wiki/Areal_features "Areal features") as well as modern lexical, structural, and grammatical similarity (over 85%) between them.[[101]](#cite_note-101)[[102]](#cite_note-102)[[103]](#cite_note-103)[[104]](#cite_note-104)[[105]](#cite_note-105) Many young Portuguese speakers however, will often choose [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") as the lingua-franca of choice, with Spanish, French, Italian, other Europeans, and speakers of other languages. Over 97% of 18 to 24 year olds in Portugal speak a second language fluently, 95% of those have English as L2, some 33.7% speak French, 23% German, and 14% Spanish.[[106]](#cite_note-106)[[107]](#cite_note-107)

[Portuñol](/wiki/Portu%C3%B1ol "Portuñol")/Portunhol, a form of [code-switching](/wiki/Code-switching "Code-switching"), has a lively use and is mentioned in popular culture in South America. Said code-switching is not to be confused with the Portuguese varieties spoken on the borders of Brazil with Uruguay (*dialeto do pampa*) and Paraguay (*dialeto dos [brasiguaios](/wiki/Brasiguayos "Brasiguayos")*), and of Portugal with Spain (*[barranquenho](/wiki/Barranquenho "Barranquenho")*), that are Portuguese dialects spoken natively by thousands of people, which have been heavily influenced by Spanish.[[108]](#cite_note-Lipski-108)

Portuguese and Spanish are the only Ibero-Romance languages, and perhaps the only Romance languages in Latin-America, with such thriving inter-language forms, in which visible and lively bilingual contact dialects and code-switching have formed, in which functional bilingual communication is achieved through attempting an approximation to the target foreign language (known as 'Portuñol') without a learned acquisition process, but nevertheless facilitates communication. There is an emerging literature focused on such phenomena in South America (including informal attempts of standardization of the linguistic continua and their usage).[[108]](#cite_note-Lipski-108)

### Galician–Portuguese in Spain

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_language&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Galician–Portuguese in Spain")]

See also: [Reintegrationism](/wiki/Reintegrationism "Reintegrationism")

The closest relative of Portuguese is Galician, which is spoken in the autonomous community and historical nationality of Galicia ([Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain")). The two were part of a common dialect continuum during the Middle Ages, known today as [Galician–Portuguese](/wiki/Galician%E2%80%93Portuguese "Galician–Portuguese"), but they have diverged especially in pronunciation and vocabulary due to the political separation of [Portugal](/wiki/County_of_Portugal "County of Portugal") from [Galicia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Galicia "Kingdom of Galicia"). There is, however, still a linguistic continuity consisting of the variant of Galician referred to as *galego-português baixo-limiao*, which is spoken in several Galician and Portuguese villages within the transboundary biosphere reserve of [Gerês](/wiki/Peneda-Ger%C3%AAs_National_Park "Peneda-Gerês National Park")-[Xurés](/wiki/Baixa_Limia_%E2%80%93_Serra_do_Xur%C3%A9s "Baixa Limia – Serra do Xurés"). It is "considered a rarity, a living vestige of the medieval language that ranged from [Cantabria](/wiki/Cantabria "Cantabria") to [Mondego](/wiki/Mondego_River "Mondego River") [...]".[[109]](#cite_note-agal-gz.or-109)
As reported by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO"), due to the pressure of Spanish on the standard official version of Galician and centuries-old [Castilianization](/wiki/Hispanization "Hispanization"), the Galician language was on the verge of disappearing.[[109]](#cite_note-agal-gz.or-109)

According to the UNESCO philologist Tapani Salminen, the proximity to Portuguese protects Galician.[[110]](#cite_note-110) The core vocabulary and grammar of Galician are noticeably closer to Portuguese than to those of Spanish. Within the EU, Galician, while not being a [European Parliament](/wiki/European_Parliament "European Parliament") official language, can be used and is in fact used by some European Parliament constituents due to its similarity with Portuguese.[[111]](#cite_note-111)[[112]](#cite_note-moniz1-112) Galician like Portuguese, uses the future subjunctive, the personal infinitive, and the synthetic pluperfect. Mutual intelligibility estimated at 85% is excellent between Galicians and Portuguese.[[113]](#cite_note-113) Despite political efforts in Spain to define them as separate languages, many linguists consider [Galician](/wiki/Galician_language "Galician language") and Portuguese to be co-dialects of the same language with regional variations.[[114]](#cite_note-114)[[112]](#cite_note-moniz1-112)

Another member of the Galician–Portuguese group, most commonly thought of as a Galician dialect, is spoken in the [Eonavian](/wiki/Galician-Asturian "Galician-Asturian") region in a western strip in [Asturias](/wiki/Asturias "Asturias") and the westernmost parts of the provinces of [León](/wiki/Province_of_Le%C3%B3n "Province of León") and [Zamora](/wiki/Province_of_Zamora "Province of Zamora"), along the frontier with Galicia, between the [Eo](/wiki/Eo_(river) "Eo (river)") and [Navia](/wiki/Navia_(river) "Navia (river)") rivers (or more exactly Eo and Frexulfe rivers). It is called *eonaviego* or *gallego-asturiano* by its speakers.

The Fala language, known by its speakers as *xalimés*, *mañegu*, *a fala de Xálima* and *chapurráu* and in Portuguese as *a fala de Xálima*, *a fala da Estremadura*, *o galego da Estremadura*, *valego* or *galaico-estremenho*, is another descendant of Galician–Portuguese, spoken by a small number of people in the Spanish towns of [Valverde del Fresno](/wiki/Valverde_del_Fresno "Valverde del Fresno") (*Valverdi du Fresnu*), [Eljas](/wiki/Eljas "Eljas") (*As Ellas*) and [San Martín de Trevejo](/wiki/San_Mart%C3%ADn_de_Trevejo "San Martín de Trevejo") (*Sa Martín de Trevellu*) in the autonomous community of [Extremadura](/wiki/Extremadura "Extremadura"), near the border with Portugal.

There are a number of other places in Spain in which the native language of the common people is a descendant of the Galician–Portuguese group, such as [La Alamedilla](/wiki/La_Alamedilla "La Alamedilla"), [Cedillo](/wiki/Cedillo "Cedillo") (*Cedilho*), [Herrera de Alcántara](/wiki/Herrera_de_Alc%C3%A1ntara "Herrera de Alcántara") (*Ferreira d'Alcântara*) and [Olivenza](/wiki/Olivenza "Olivenza") (*Olivença*), but in these municipalities, what is spoken is actually Portuguese, not disputed as such in the mainstream.

The diversity of dialects of the Portuguese language is known since the time of medieval Portuguese-Galician language when it coexisted with the Lusitanian-Mozarabic dialect, spoken in the south of Portugal. The dialectal diversity becomes more evident in the work of [Fernão d'Oliveira](/wiki/Fern%C3%A3o_de_Oliveira "Fernão de Oliveira"), in the *Grammatica da Lingoagem Portuguesa*, (1536), where he remarks that the people of Portuguese regions of Beira, Alentejo, Estremadura, and Entre Douro e Minho, all speak differently from each other. Also Contador d'Argote (1725) distinguishes three main varieties of dialects: the local dialects, the dialects of time, and of profession (work jargon). Of local dialects he highlights five main dialects: the dialect of Estremadura, of Entre-Douro e Minho, of Beira, of Algarve and of Trás-os-Montes. He also makes reference to the overseas dialects, the rustic dialects, the poetic dialect and that of prose.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In the kingdom of Portugal, *Ladinho* (or *Lingoagem Ladinha*) was the name given to the pure Portuguese romance language, without any mixture of Aravia or Gerigonça Judenga.[[115]](#cite_note-115) While the term *língua vulgar* was used to name the language before D. Dinis decided to call it "Portuguese language",[[116]](#cite_note-116) the erudite version used and known as Galician–Portuguese (the language of the Portuguese court) and all other Portuguese dialects were spoken at the same time. In a historical perspective the Portuguese language was never just one dialect. Just like today there is a standard Portuguese (actually two) among the several dialects of Portuguese, in the past there was Galician–Portuguese as the "standard", coexisting with other dialects.

### Influence on other languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_language&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Influence on other languages")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/St_Peter_Church_Melaka_3.jpg/250px-St_Peter_Church_Melaka_3.jpg)](/wiki/File:St_Peter_Church_Melaka_3.jpg)

An Old Portuguese [memento mori](/wiki/Memento_mori "Memento mori") memorial sign in [Malacca City](/wiki/Malacca_City "Malacca City")

See also: [List of English words of Portuguese origin](/wiki/List_of_English_words_of_Portuguese_origin "List of English words of Portuguese origin"), [Loan words in Indonesian](/wiki/Loan_words_in_Indonesian "Loan words in Indonesian"), [Japanese words of Portuguese origin](/wiki/Japanese_words_of_Portuguese_origin "Japanese words of Portuguese origin"), [List of Malay loanwords](/wiki/List_of_Malay_loanwords "List of Malay loanwords"), [Portuguese loanwords in Sinhala](/wiki/Portuguese_loanwords_in_Sinhala "Portuguese loanwords in Sinhala"), [Loan words in Sri Lankan Tamil § Portuguese](/wiki/Loan_words_in_Sri_Lankan_Tamil#Portuguese "Loan words in Sri Lankan Tamil"), [Sri Lanka Indo-Portuguese language](/wiki/Sri_Lanka_Indo-Portuguese_language "Sri Lanka Indo-Portuguese language"), [Hindustani etymology § Loanwords from Portuguese](/wiki/Hindustani_etymology#Loanwords_from_Portuguese "Hindustani etymology"), [Gujarati language § Portuguese](/wiki/Gujarati_language#Portuguese "Gujarati language"), [Burmese language](/wiki/Burmese_language "Burmese language"), [Bengali vocabulary § Portuguese (পর্তুগিজ Pôrtugij)](/wiki/Bengali_vocabulary#Portuguese_(পর্তুগিজ_Pôrtugij) "Bengali vocabulary"), [Thai language § Portuguese-origin](/wiki/Thai_language#Portuguese-origin "Thai language"), [Chittagonian language](/wiki/Chittagonian_language "Chittagonian language"), and [Tok Pisin](/wiki/Tok_Pisin "Tok Pisin")

Portuguese has provided [loanwords](/wiki/Loanword "Loanword") to many languages, such as [Indonesian](/wiki/Indonesian_language "Indonesian language"), [Manado Malay](/wiki/Manado_Malay "Manado Malay"), [Malayalam](/wiki/Malayalam "Malayalam"), [Sri Lankan Tamil](/wiki/Sri_Lanka_Tamils_(native) "Sri Lanka Tamils (native)") and [Sinhala](/wiki/Sinhala_language "Sinhala language"), [Malay](/wiki/Malay_language "Malay language"), [Bengali](/wiki/Bengali_language "Bengali language"), [English](/wiki/English_(language) "English (language)"), [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi "Hindi"), [Swahili](/wiki/Swahili_language "Swahili language"), [Afrikaans](/wiki/Afrikaans "Afrikaans"), [Konkani](/wiki/Konkani_language "Konkani language"), [Marathi](/wiki/Marathi_language "Marathi language"), [Punjabi](/wiki/Punjabi_language "Punjabi language"), [Tetum](/wiki/Tetum_language "Tetum language"), [Xitsonga](/wiki/Tsonga_language "Tsonga language"), [Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_language "Japanese language"), [Lanc-Patuá](/wiki/Lanc-Patu%C3%A1_creole "Lanc-Patuá creole"), [Esan](/wiki/Esan_people#Language "Esan people"), [Bandari](/wiki/Bandar_Abbas "Bandar Abbas") (spoken in Iran) and [Sranan Tongo](/wiki/Sranan_Tongo "Sranan Tongo") (spoken in Suriname). It left a strong influence on the *[língua brasílica](/wiki/Old_Tupi "Old Tupi")*, a [Tupi–Guarani language](/wiki/Tupi%E2%80%93Guarani_language "Tupi–Guarani language"), which was the most widely spoken in Brazil until the 18th century, and on the language spoken around [Sikka](/wiki/Sikka_Regency "Sikka Regency") in [Flores](/wiki/Flores "Flores") Island, [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"). In nearby [Larantuka](/wiki/Larantuka "Larantuka"), Portuguese is used for prayers in [Holy Week](/wiki/Holy_Week "Holy Week") rituals.
The Japanese–Portuguese dictionary *[Nippo Jisho](/wiki/Nippo_Jisho "Nippo Jisho")* (1603) was the first dictionary of Japanese in a European language, a product of [Jesuit](/wiki/Society_of_Jesus "Society of Jesus") missionary activity in Japan. Building on the work of earlier Portuguese missionaries, the *[Dictionarium Anamiticum, Lusitanum et Latinum](/wiki/Dictionarium_Anamiticum,_Lusitanum_et_Latinum "Dictionarium Anamiticum, Lusitanum et Latinum")* (Annamite–Portuguese–Latin dictionary) of [Alexandre de Rhodes](/wiki/Alexandre_de_Rhodes "Alexandre de Rhodes") (1651) introduced the modern [orthography of Vietnamese](/wiki/Vietnamese_alphabet "Vietnamese alphabet"), which is based on the orthography of 17th-century Portuguese. The [Romanization](/wiki/Romanization "Romanization") of [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language") was also influenced by the Portuguese language (among others), particularly regarding [Chinese surnames](/wiki/List_of_common_Chinese_surnames "List of common Chinese surnames"); one example is *Mei*. During 1583–88 Italian Jesuits [Michele Ruggieri](/wiki/Michele_Ruggieri "Michele Ruggieri") and [Matteo Ricci](/wiki/Matteo_Ricci "Matteo Ricci") created a Portuguese–Chinese dictionary – the first ever European–Chinese dictionary.[[117]](#cite_note-camus-117)[[118]](#cite_note-ricciDict-118)

For instance, as [Portuguese merchants](/wiki/Portuguese_Empire "Portuguese Empire") were presumably the first to introduce the [sweet orange](/wiki/Sweet_orange "Sweet orange") in Europe, in several modern [Indo-European languages](/wiki/Indo-European_languages "Indo-European languages") the fruit has been named after them. Some examples are Albanian *[portokall](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/portokall#Albanian "wikt:portokall")*, Bosnian (archaic) *portokal*, *prtokal*, Bulgarian [портокал](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%D0%BF%D0%BE%D1%80%D1%82%D0%BE%D0%BA%D0%B0%D0%BB#Bulgarian "wikt:портокал") (*portokal*), Greek [πορτοκάλι](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%CF%80%CE%BF%CF%81%CF%84%CE%BF%CE%BA%CE%AC%CE%BB%CE%B9#Greek "wikt:πορτοκάλι") (*portokáli*), [Macedonian](/wiki/Macedonian_language "Macedonian language") **portokal**, Persian [پرتقال](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%D9%BE%D8%B1%D8%AA%D9%82%D8%A7%D9%84#Persian "wikt:پرتقال") (*porteghal*), and Romanian *[portocală](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/portocal%C4%83#Romanian "wikt:portocală")*.[[119]](#cite_note-plantname-119)[[120]](#cite_note-OstergrenBosse2011-120) Related names can be found in other languages, such as Arabic [البرتقال](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%D8%A7%D9%84%D8%A8%D8%B1%D8%AA%D9%82%D8%A7%D9%84#Arabic "wikt:البرتقال") (*burtuqāl*), [Georgian](/wiki/Georgian_language "Georgian language") [ფორთოხალი](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E1%83%A4%E1%83%9D%E1%83%A0%E1%83%97%E1%83%9D%E1%83%AE%E1%83%90%E1%83%9A%E1%83%98#Georgian "wikt:ფორთოხალი") (*p'ort'oxali*), Turkish *[portakal](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/portakal#Turkish "wikt:portakal")* and [Amharic](/wiki/Amharic "Amharic") *birtukan*.[[119]](#cite_note-plantname-119) Also, in southern [Italian dialects](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language") (e.g. [Neapolitan](/wiki/Neapolitan_language "Neapolitan language")), an orange is *[portogallo](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/portogallo "wikt:portogallo")* or *[purtuallo](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/it:purtuallo "wikt:it:purtuallo")*, literally "(the) Portuguese (one)", in contrast to [standard Italian](/wiki/Standard_Italian "Standard Italian") *arancia*.

### Derived languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_language&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Derived languages")]

Main article: [Portuguese-based creole languages](/wiki/Portuguese-based_creole_languages "Portuguese-based creole languages")

Beginning in the 16th century, the extensive contacts between Portuguese travelers and settlers, African and Asian slaves, and local populations led to the appearance of many [pidgins](/wiki/Pidgin "Pidgin") with varying amounts of Portuguese influence.

As each of these pidgins became the mother tongue of succeeding generations, they evolved into fully fledged [creole languages](/wiki/Creole_language "Creole language"), which remained in use in many parts of Asia, Africa and South America until the 18th century.

Some Portuguese-based or Portuguese-influenced creoles are still spoken today, namely [Cape Verdean Creole](/wiki/Cape_Verdean_Creole "Cape Verdean Creole") and [Papiamento](/wiki/Papiamento "Papiamento"). Portuguese-based creoles are spoken by over three million people worldwide, especially people of partial [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_people "Portuguese people") ancestry.

## Language structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_language&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Language structure")]

### Phonology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_language&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Phonology")]

Main article: [Portuguese phonology](/wiki/Portuguese_phonology "Portuguese phonology")

Sara, a native speaker of [European Portuguese](/wiki/European_Portuguese "European Portuguese")



Spoken [Brazilian Portuguese](/wiki/Brazilian_Portuguese "Brazilian Portuguese")

Portuguese phonology is similar to those of languages such as [Franco-Provençal](/wiki/Franco-Proven%C3%A7al_language "Franco-Provençal language") and [Catalan](/wiki/Catalan_phonology "Catalan phonology"), whereas [that of Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_phonology "Spanish phonology") is similar to those of [Sardinian](/wiki/Sardinian_language "Sardinian language") and the [Southern Italian dialects](/wiki/Neapolitan_language "Neapolitan language"). Some would describe the phonology of Portuguese as a blend of [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"), [Gallo-Romance](/wiki/Gallo-Romance_languages "Gallo-Romance languages") (e.g. [French](/wiki/French_language "French language")) and the [languages of northern Italy](/wiki/Gallo-Italic_languages "Gallo-Italic languages") (especially [Genoese](/wiki/Genoese_dialect "Genoese dialect")).[[121]](#cite_note-121)[[10]](#cite_note-Gramatica_2007-10)

Portuguese can have as many as nine oral vowels, as many as two semivowels, and as many as 21 consonants; some varieties of the language have fewer [phonemes](/wiki/Phoneme "Phoneme"). There are also five [nasal vowels](/wiki/Nasal_vowel "Nasal vowel"), which some linguists regard as [allophones](/wiki/Allophone "Allophone") of oral vowels.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Galician–Portuguese developed in the region of the former Roman province of [Gallaecia](/wiki/Gallaecia "Gallaecia"), from the [Vulgar Latin](/wiki/Vulgar_Latin "Vulgar Latin") (common Latin) that had been introduced by Roman soldiers, colonists and magistrates during the time of the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire"). Although the process may have been slower than in other regions, after a period of bilingualism, the centuries of contact with Vulgar Latin completely extinguished the native languages, and a variety of Latin with a few Gallaecian features evolved.[[122]](#cite_note-122)[[123]](#cite_note-123)

[Gallaecian](/wiki/Gallaecian_language "Gallaecian language") and [Lusitanian](/wiki/Lusitanian_language "Lusitanian language") influences were absorbed into the local dialect of Vulgar Latin; this can be detected in some Galician–Portuguese words, as well as in placenames of [Celtic](/wiki/Celtic_language "Celtic language") and [Iberian](/wiki/Iberians "Iberians") origin.[[124]](#cite_note-124)[[125]](#cite_note-125)

An early form of Galician–Portuguese was already spoken in the [Kingdom of the Suebi](/wiki/Kingdom_of_the_Suebi "Kingdom of the Suebi"), and by the year 800 Galician–Portuguese had already become the [vernacular](/wiki/Vernacular "Vernacular") of northwestern Iberia.[[126]](#cite_note-lus-126) The first known [phonetic](/wiki/Phonetic "Phonetic") changes in Vulgar Latin, which began the evolution to Galician–Portuguese, took place during the rule of the Germanic groups, the [Suebi](/wiki/Suebi "Suebi") (411–585) and [Visigoths](/wiki/Visigoths "Visigoths") (585–711).[[126]](#cite_note-lus-126) The Galician–Portuguese "inflected infinitive" (or "personal infinitive")[[127]](#cite_note-127)[[128]](#cite_note-128) and the [nasal vowels](/wiki/Nasal_vowels "Nasal vowels") may have evolved under the influence of local [Celtic](/wiki/Celtic_languages "Celtic languages") (as in [Old French](/wiki/Old_French "Old French")).[[12]](#cite_note-cornell-12)[[129]](#cite_note-129) The nasal vowels would thus be a phonologic characteristic of the Vulgar Latin spoken in Roman [Gallaecia](/wiki/Gallaecia "Gallaecia"), but they are not attested in writing until after the 6th and 7th centuries.[[130]](#cite_note-130)

Unstressed isolated vowels tend to be [raised](/wiki/Vowel_height "Vowel height") and sometimes centralized.

**Phonetic notes**

* Semivowels contrast with unstressed high vowels in verbal conjugation, as in *(eu) rio* /ˈʁi.u/ and *(ele) riu* /ˈʁiw/.[[139]](#cite_note-139) Phonologists discuss whether their nature is vowel or consonant.[[140]](#cite_note-140)
* In most of Brazil and Angola, the consonant hereafter denoted as /ɲ/ is realized as a [nasal palatal approximant](/wiki/Nasal_palatal_approximant "Nasal palatal approximant") [[j̃](/wiki/Nasalized_voiced_palatal_approximant "Nasalized voiced palatal approximant")], which [nasalizes](/wiki/Nasalization "Nasalization") the vowel that precedes it: [ˈnĩj̃u].[[141]](#cite_note-141)[[142]](#cite_note-142)
* [Bisol (2005](#CITEREFBisol2005):122) proposes that Portuguese possesses labio-velar stops /kʷ/ and /ɡʷ/ as additional phonemes rather than sequences of a velar stop and /w/.[[138]](#cite_note-labialized_velar_-_Bisol-138)
* The consonant hereafter denoted as /ʁ/ has a variety of realizations depending on dialect. In Europe, it is typically a [uvular trill](/wiki/Uvular_trill "Uvular trill") [ʀ]; however, a pronunciation as a [voiced uvular fricative](/wiki/Voiced_uvular_fricative "Voiced uvular fricative") [ʁ] may be becoming dominant in urban areas. There is also a realization as a [voiceless uvular fricative](/wiki/Voiceless_uvular_fricative "Voiceless uvular fricative") [χ], and the original pronunciation as an [alveolar trill](/wiki/Alveolar_trill "Alveolar trill") [r] also remains very common in various dialects.[[143]](#cite_note-143) A common realization of the word-initial /r/ in the Lisbon accent is a voiced uvular fricative trill [[ʀ̝](/wiki/Voiced_uvular_fricative_trill "Voiced uvular fricative trill")].[[144]](#cite_note-gr157-144) In Brazil, /ʁ/ can be [velar](/wiki/Velar_consonant "Velar consonant"), [uvular](/wiki/Uvular_consonant "Uvular consonant"), or [glottal](/wiki/Glottal_consonant "Glottal consonant") and may be voiceless unless between voiced sounds.[[145]](#cite_note-145) It is usually pronounced as a [voiceless velar fricative](/wiki/Voiceless_velar_fricative "Voiceless velar fricative") [x], a [voiceless glottal fricative](/wiki/Voiceless_glottal_fricative "Voiceless glottal fricative") [h] or [voiceless uvular fricative](/wiki/Voiceless_uvular_fricative "Voiceless uvular fricative") [χ]. See also [Guttural R § Portuguese](/wiki/Guttural_R#Portuguese "Guttural R").
* /s/ and /z/ are normally [lamino-alveolar](/wiki/Lamino-alveolar_consonant "Lamino-alveolar consonant"), as in English. However, a number of dialects in northern Portugal pronounce /s/ and /z/ as [apico-alveolar](/wiki/Apico-alveolar "Apico-alveolar") sibilants (sounding somewhat like a soft [ʃ] or [ʒ]), as in the Romance languages of northern Iberia. Some very few northeastern Portugal dialects still maintain the medieval distinction between apical and laminal [sibilants](/wiki/Sibilant "Sibilant") (written *s/ss* and *c/ç/z*, respectively).
* As a phoneme, /tʃ/ occurs only in loanwords, names, and interjections, with a dialectal tendency for speakers to substitute in /ʃ/ in most dialects outside of Brazil (as well as some conservative Brazilian dialects, to a variable extent.) However, [tʃ] is an [allophone](/wiki/Allophone "Allophone") of /t/ before /i/ in a majority of Brazilian dialects. Similarly, [dʒ] is an allophone of /d/ in the same contexts.
* In northern and central Portugal, the voiced stops (/b/, /d/, and /ɡ/) are usually lenited to [fricatives](/wiki/Fricative "Fricative") [β], [ð], and [ɣ], respectively, except at the beginning of words or after nasal vowels.[[132]](#cite_note-Cruz-Ferreira92-132)[[133]](#cite_note-mateus11-133) At the end of a phrase, due to [final-obstruent devoicing](/wiki/Final-obstruent_devoicing "Final-obstruent devoicing"), they may even be devoiced to [ɸ], [θ], and [x] (for example, *verde* at the end of a sentence may be pronounced [veɹθ]).
* In Brazil, many speakers further shift [ɫ] to [w] in closed syllables, especially outside the southern region.
* Phonetically, Portuguese and [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"), are quite different from the other major Romance languages. It has been suggested that this stems from the ancient link to Celtic languages such as [Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language") or [Breton](/wiki/Breton_language "Breton language"),[[146]](#cite_note-146) with which it also shares a substantial number of cognates: there are 37 sounds in Portuguese, including vowels, consonants and diphthongs, most of which exist in today's [Celtic languages](/wiki/Celtic_languages "Celtic languages").[[147]](#cite_note-Filppula-Klemola-147)

### Orthography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_language&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Orthography")]

These paragraphs are an excerpt from [Portuguese orthography](/wiki/Portuguese_orthography "Portuguese orthography").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_orthography&action=edit)]

[Portuguese orthography](/wiki/Portuguese_orthography "Portuguese orthography") is based on the [Latin alphabet](/wiki/Latin_alphabet "Latin alphabet") and makes use of the [acute accent](/wiki/Acute_accent "Acute accent"), the [circumflex accent](/wiki/Circumflex_accent "Circumflex accent"), the [grave accent](/wiki/Grave_accent "Grave accent"), the [tilde](/wiki/Tilde "Tilde"), and the [cedilla](/wiki/Cedilla "Cedilla") to denote stress, [vowel height](/wiki/Vowel_height "Vowel height"), nasalization, and other sound changes. The [diaeresis](/wiki/Diaeresis_(diacritic) "Diaeresis (diacritic)") was abolished by the last [Orthography Agreement](/wiki/Portuguese_Language_Orthographic_Agreement_of_1990 "Portuguese Language Orthographic Agreement of 1990"). Accented letters and digraphs are not counted as separate characters for [collation](/wiki/Collation "Collation") purposes.

The spelling of Portuguese is largely [phonemic](/wiki/Phonemic_orthography "Phonemic orthography"), but some [phonemes](/wiki/Phoneme "Phoneme") can be spelled in more than one way. In ambiguous cases, the correct spelling is determined through a combination of [etymology](/wiki/Etymology "Etymology") with [morphology](/wiki/Morphology_(linguistics) "Morphology (linguistics)") and tradition; so there is not a perfect one-to-one correspondence between sounds and letters or digraphs. Knowing the main [inflectional](/wiki/Inflection "Inflection") paradigms of Portuguese and being acquainted with the orthography of other Western European languages can be helpful.
