### Grammar

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_language&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Grammar")]

Main article: [Portuguese grammar](/wiki/Portuguese_grammar "Portuguese grammar")

A notable aspect of the grammar of Portuguese is the verb. Morphologically, more verbal inflections from classical Latin have been preserved by Portuguese than by any other major [Romance language](/wiki/Romance_language "Romance language"). Portuguese and Spanish share very similar grammar, vocabulary and sentence structure. Portuguese also has some grammatical innovations not found in other Romance languages (except Galician and Fala):

* The [present perfect](/wiki/Present_perfect "Present perfect") has an iterative sense unique to the Galician–Portuguese language group. It denotes an action or a series of actions that began in the past but expected to occur again in the future. For instance, the sentence *Tenho tentado falar contigo* would be translated to "I have been trying to talk to you", not "I have tried to talk to you." On the other hand, the correct translation of "Have you heard the latest news?" is not *\*Tens ouvido as últimas?* but *Ouviste as últimas?* since no repetition is implied.[[148]](#cite_note-148)
* Portuguese makes use of the future [subjunctive mood](/wiki/Subjunctive_mood "Subjunctive mood"), which developed from medieval [West Iberian Romance](/wiki/West_Iberian_languages "West Iberian languages"). In modern Spanish and Galician, it has almost entirely fallen into disuse. The future subjunctive appears in dependent clauses that denote a condition that must be fulfilled in the future so that the independent clause will occur. English normally employs the present tense under the same circumstances:

:   *Se eu* for *eleito presidente, mudarei a lei.*
:   If *I am* elected president, I will change the law.

:   *Quando* fores *mais velho, vais entender.*
:   When *you grow* older, you will understand.

* The personal [infinitive](/wiki/Infinitive "Infinitive") can [inflect](/wiki/Inflection "Inflection") according to its subject in [person](/wiki/Grammatical_person "Grammatical person") and [number](/wiki/Grammatical_number "Grammatical number"). It often shows who is expected to perform a certain action. *É melhor voltares* "It is better [for you] to go back", *É melhor voltarmos* "It is better [for us] to go back." Perhaps for that reason, infinitive clauses replace subjunctive clauses more often in Portuguese than in other Romance languages.

### Vocabulary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_language&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Vocabulary")]

Main article: [Portuguese vocabulary](/wiki/Portuguese_vocabulary "Portuguese vocabulary")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/f/f3/Languages_of_pre-Roman_Iberia.gif/250px-Languages_of_pre-Roman_Iberia.gif)](/wiki/File:Languages_of_pre-Roman_Iberia.gif)

Linguistic map of Pre-Roman Iberia

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Pal%C3%A1cio_Presidencial_em_Bissau_%281%29.jpg/250px-Pal%C3%A1cio_Presidencial_em_Bissau_%281%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pal%C3%A1cio_Presidencial_em_Bissau_(1).jpg)

The [Bissau-Guinean Presidential Palace](/wiki/Presidential_Palace,_Bissau "Presidential Palace, Bissau"), with its [Portuguese colonial architecture](/wiki/Portuguese_colonial_architecture "Portuguese colonial architecture"), is a building that has a library, a small theater and was formerly the palace of the colonial governor of [Portuguese-Guinea](/wiki/Portuguese_Guinea "Portuguese Guinea"), seen from the PAIGC-building (formerly the seat of the local commercial association *Associação Comercial, Industrial e Agrícola de Bissau*), located at the *Praça dos Heróis Nacionais* square (formerly *Praça do Império square*), in downtown [Bissau](/wiki/Bissau "Bissau").

Most of the lexicon of Portuguese is derived, directly or through other Romance languages, from Latin. Nevertheless, because of its original [Lusitanian](/wiki/Lusitanians "Lusitanians") and Celtic [Gallaecian](/wiki/Gallaeci "Gallaeci") heritage, and the later participation of Portugal in the [Age of Discovery](/wiki/Age_of_Discovery "Age of Discovery"), it has a relevant number of words from the ancient [Hispano-Celtic group](/wiki/Hispano-Celtic_languages "Hispano-Celtic languages")[[11]](#cite_note-Colera_2007_p.750_quote-11) and adopted [loanwords](/wiki/Loanword "Loanword") from other languages around the world.

A number of Portuguese words can still be traced to the [pre-Roman inhabitants of Portugal](/wiki/Pre-Roman_peoples_of_the_Iberian_Peninsula "Pre-Roman peoples of the Iberian Peninsula"), which included the [Gallaeci](/wiki/Gallaeci "Gallaeci"), [Lusitanians](/wiki/Lusitanians "Lusitanians"), [Celtici](/wiki/Celtici "Celtici") and [Cynetes](/wiki/Cynetes "Cynetes"). Most of these words derived from the Hispano-Celtic [Gallaecian language](/wiki/Gallaecian_language "Gallaecian language") of northwestern Iberia, and are very often shared with [Galician](/wiki/Galician_language "Galician language") since both languages have the same origin in the medieval language of Galician–Portuguese. A few of these words existed in Latin as loanwords from other [Celtic](/wiki/Celtic_languages "Celtic languages") sources, often [Gaulish](/wiki/Gaulish "Gaulish"). Altogether these are over 3,000 words, verbs, toponymic names of towns, rivers, surnames, tools, lexicon linked to rural life and natural world.[[147]](#cite_note-Filppula-Klemola-147)

In the 5th century, the Iberian Peninsula (the Roman [Hispania](/wiki/Hispania "Hispania")) was conquered by the [Germanic](/wiki/Germanic_peoples "Germanic peoples"), [Suebi](/wiki/Suebi "Suebi") and [Visigoths](/wiki/Visigoths "Visigoths"). As they adopted the [Roman](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome") civilization and language, however, these people contributed with some 500
[Germanic](/wiki/Germanic_languages "Germanic languages") words to the lexicon. Many of these words are related to:

* **warfare**, such as *espora* 'spur', *estaca* ('stake'), and *guerra* ('war'), from [Gothic](/wiki/Gothic_language "Gothic language") *\*spaúra*, *\*stakka*, and *\*wirro* respectively;
* **natural world**, such as *suino* ('swine') from *\*sweina*, *gavião* ('hawk') from *\*gabilans*, *vaga* ('spot') from *\*vigan*;
* **human emotions**, such as *orgulho* or *orgulhoso* ('pride', 'proud') from Old Germanic *\*urguol*, and
* **verbs** like *gravar* ('to craft, record, graft') from *\*graba* or *esmagar* ('to squeeze, quash, grind') from Suebian *\*magōn* or *esfarrapar* ('to shred') from *\*harpō*.

The [Germanic languages](/wiki/Germanic_languages "Germanic languages") influence also exists in [toponymic surnames](/wiki/Toponymic_surname "Toponymic surname") and [patronymic surnames](/wiki/Patronymic_surname "Patronymic surname") borne by Visigoth sovereigns and their descendants, and it dwells on placenames such as [Ermesinde](/wiki/Ermesinde "Ermesinde"), [Esposende](/wiki/Esposende "Esposende") and [Resende](/wiki/Resende,_Portugal "Resende, Portugal") where *sinde* and *sende* are derived from the Germanic *sinths* ('military expedition') and in the case of Resende, the prefix *re* comes from Germanic *reths* ('council'). Other examples of Portuguese names, surnames and town names of Germanic [toponymic](/wiki/Toponymic "Toponymic") origin include Henrique, [Henriques](/wiki/Henriques_(surname) "Henriques (surname)"), Vermoim, Mandim, Calquim, Baguim, Gemunde, Guetim, Sermonde and many more, are quite common mainly in the old [Suebi](/wiki/Suebi "Suebi") and later [Visigothic](/wiki/Visigothic "Visigothic") dominated regions, covering today's Northern half of Portugal and [Galicia](/wiki/Galicia_(Spain) "Galicia (Spain)").

Between the 9th and early 13th centuries, Portuguese acquired some 400 to 600 words from [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language") by influence of [Moorish Iberia](/wiki/Al-Andalus "Al-Andalus"). They are often recognizable by the initial [Arabic article *a(l)-*](/wiki/Definite_article_in_Arabic "Definite article in Arabic"), and include common words such as *aldeia* ('village') from الضيعة *aḍ-ḍayʿa*, *alface* ('lettuce') from الخسة *al-khassa*, *armazém* ('warehouse') from المخزن *al-makhzan*, and *azeite* ('olive oil') from الزيت *az-zayt*.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/State_Central_Library%2C_Goa_Dec_27%2C_2012_14.JPG/250px-State_Central_Library%2C_Goa_Dec_27%2C_2012_14.JPG)](/wiki/File:State_Central_Library,_Goa_Dec_27,_2012_14.JPG)

A sign at [Goa Central Library](/wiki/Goa_State_Central_Library "Goa State Central Library"), in [Panaji](/wiki/Panaji "Panaji"), India, listing three Portuguese-language newspapers

Starting in the 15th century, the Portuguese maritime explorations led to the introduction of many loanwords from Asian languages. For instance, *catana* ('[cutlass](/wiki/Cutlass "Cutlass")') from [Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_language "Japanese language") *[katana](/wiki/Katana "Katana")*, *chá* ('tea') from [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language") *[chá](/wiki/Tea#Etymology "Tea")*, and *[canja](/wiki/Canja_de_galinha "Canja de galinha")*[[149]](#cite_note-149) ('chicken-soup, piece of cake') from [Malayalam](/wiki/Malayalam "Malayalam").

.

From the 16th to the 19th centuries, because of the role of Portugal as intermediary in the [Atlantic slave trade](/wiki/Atlantic_slave_trade "Atlantic slave trade"), and the establishment of large Portuguese colonies in Angola, Mozambique, and Brazil, Portuguese acquired several words of African and [Amerind](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_Brazil "Indigenous peoples of Brazil") origin, especially names for most of the animals and plants found in those territories. While those terms are mostly used in the former colonies, many became current in European Portuguese as well. From [Kimbundu](/wiki/Kimbundu_language "Kimbundu language"), for example, came *kifumate* > *cafuné* ('head caress') (Brazil), *kusula* > *caçula* ('youngest child') (Brazil), *marimbondo* ('tropical wasp') (Brazil), and *kubungula* > *bungular* ('to dance like a wizard') (Angola). From South America came *batata* ('[potato](/wiki/Potato "Potato")'), from [Taino](/wiki/Ta%C3%ADno_language "Taíno language"); *ananás* and *abacaxi*, from [Tupi–Guarani](/wiki/Tupi%E2%80%93Guarani_languages "Tupi–Guarani languages") *naná* and [Tupi](/wiki/Tupi_language "Tupi language") *ibá cati*, respectively (two species of [pineapple](/wiki/Pineapple "Pineapple")), and *pipoca* ('[popcorn](/wiki/Popcorn "Popcorn")') from Tupi and *tucano* ('[toucan](/wiki/Toucan "Toucan")') from [Guarani](/wiki/Guarani_language "Guarani language") *tucan*.

Finally, it has received a steady influx of loanwords from other European languages, especially French and [English](/wiki/English_language "English language"). These are by far the most important languages when referring to loanwords. There are many examples such as: *colchete*/*crochê* ('bracket'/'crochet'), *paletó* ('jacket'), *batom* ('lipstick'), and *filé*/*filete* ('steak'/'slice'), *rua* ('street'), respectively, from French *crochet*, *paletot*, *bâton*, *filet*, *rue*; and *bife* ('steak'), *futebol*, *revólver*, *stock*/*estoque*, *folclore*, from English "beef", "football", "revolver", "stock", "folklore".

Examples from other European languages: *macarrão* ('pasta'), *piloto* ('pilot'), *carroça* ('carriage'), and *barraca* ('barrack'), from Italian *maccherone*, *pilota*, *carrozza*, and *baracca*; *melena* ('hair lock'), *fiambre* ('wet-cured ham') (in Portugal, in contrast with *presunto* 'dry-cured ham' from Latin *prae-exsuctus* 'dehydrated') or ('canned ham') (in Brazil, in contrast with non-canned, wet-cured (*presunto cozido*) and dry-cured (*presunto cru*)), or *castelhano* ('Castilian'), from Spanish *melena* ('mane'), *fiambre* and *castellano.*

## Dialects, accents and varieties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_language&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Dialects, accents and varieties")]

Modern Standard [European Portuguese](/wiki/European_Portuguese "European Portuguese") (*português padrão*[[150]](#cite_note-150) or *português continental*) is based on the Portuguese spoken in the area including and surrounding the cities of [Coimbra](/wiki/Coimbra "Coimbra") and [Lisbon](/wiki/Lisbon "Lisbon"), in central Portugal. Standard European Portuguese is also the preferred standard by the Portuguese-speaking African countries. As such, and despite the fact that its speakers are dispersed around the world, Portuguese has only two dialects used for learning: the European and the Brazilian. Some aspects and sounds found in many dialects of Brazil are exclusive to South America, and cannot be found in Europe. The same occur with the Santomean, Mozambican, Bissau-Guinean, Angolan and Cape Verdean dialects, being exclusive to Africa. See [Portuguese in Africa](/wiki/Portuguese_in_Africa "Portuguese in Africa").

Audio samples of some dialects and accents of Portuguese are available below. There are some differences between the areas but these are the best approximations possible. IPA transcriptions refer to the names in local pronunciation.

### Brazil

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_language&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Brazil")]

1. *[Caipira](/wiki/Caipira_dialect "Caipira dialect")* – Spoken in the states of [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_(state) "São Paulo (state)") (most markedly on the countryside and rural areas); southern [Minas Gerais](/wiki/Minas_Gerais "Minas Gerais"), northern [Paraná](/wiki/Paran%C3%A1_(state) "Paraná (state)") and southeastern [Mato Grosso do Sul](/wiki/Mato_Grosso_do_Sul "Mato Grosso do Sul"). Depending on the vision of what constitutes *caipira*, [Triângulo Mineiro](/wiki/Tri%C3%A2ngulo_Mineiro "Triângulo Mineiro"), border areas of [Goiás](/wiki/Goi%C3%A1s "Goiás") and the remaining parts of Mato Grosso do Sul are included, and the frontier of *caipira* in Minas Gerais is expanded further northerly, though not reaching metropolitan [Belo Horizonte](/wiki/Belo_Horizonte "Belo Horizonte"). It is often said that *caipira* appeared by [decreolization](/wiki/Decreolization "Decreolization") of the [língua brasílica](/wiki/Old_Tupi "Old Tupi") and the related [língua geral paulista](/wiki/L%C3%ADngua_geral_paulista "Língua geral paulista"), then spoken in almost all of what is now São Paulo, a former [lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca") in most of the contemporary [Centro-Sul](/wiki/Centro-Sul "Centro-Sul") of Brazil before the 18th century, brought by the *[bandeirantes](/wiki/Bandeirante "Bandeirante")*, interior pioneers of [Colonial Brazil](/wiki/Colonial_Brazil "Colonial Brazil"), closely related to its northern counterpart [Nheengatu](/wiki/Nheengatu_language "Nheengatu language"), and that is why the dialect shows many general differences from other variants of the language.[[153]](#cite_note-153) It has striking remarkable differences in comparison to other Brazilian dialects in phonology, prosody and grammar, often [stigmatized](/wiki/Social_stigma "Social stigma") as being strongly associated with a [substandard variant](/wiki/Prestige_(sociolinguistics) "Prestige (sociolinguistics)"), now mostly rural.[[154]](#cite_note-154)[[155]](#cite_note-155)[[156]](#cite_note-156)[[157]](#cite_note-157)[[158]](#cite_note-158)
2. [*Cearense* or *Costa norte*](/wiki/North_coast_Portuguese "North coast Portuguese") – is a dialect spoken more sharply in the states of Ceará and Piauí. The variant of Ceará includes fairly distinctive traits it shares with the one spoken in Piauí, though, such as distinctive regional phonology and vocabulary (for example, a debuccalization process stronger than that of Portuguese, a different system of the vowel harmony that spans Brazil from *fluminense* and *mineiro* to *amazofonia* but is especially prevalent in *nordestino*, a very coherent coda sibilant palatalization as those of Portugal and Rio de Janeiro but allowed in fewer environments than in other accents of *nordestino*, a greater presence of dental stop palatalization to palato-alveolar in comparison to other accents of *nordestino*, among others, as well as a great number of archaic Portuguese words).[[159]](#cite_note-159)[[160]](#cite_note-160)[[161]](#cite_note-161)[[162]](#cite_note-162)[[163]](#cite_note-163)[[164]](#cite_note-164)
3. *Baiano* – Found in [Bahia](/wiki/Bahia "Bahia") and border regions with [Goiás](/wiki/Goi%C3%A1s "Goiás") and [Tocantins](/wiki/Tocantins "Tocantins"). Similar to *nordestino*, it has a very characteristic [syllable-timed rhythm](/wiki/Stress_timing "Stress timing") and the greatest tendency to pronounce unstressed vowels as open-mid [[ɛ](/wiki/Open-mid_front_unrounded_vowel "Open-mid front unrounded vowel")] and [[ɔ](/wiki/Open-mid_back_rounded_vowel "Open-mid back rounded vowel")].
4. [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Loudspeaker.svg/20px-Loudspeaker.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Loudspeaker.svg) [*Fluminense*](http://cvc.instituto-camoes.pt/hlp/geografia/som90.html) – A broad dialect with many variants spoken in the states of [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro_(state) "Rio de Janeiro (state)"), [Espírito Santo](/wiki/Esp%C3%ADrito_Santo "Espírito Santo") and neighboring eastern regions of [Minas Gerais](/wiki/Minas_Gerais "Minas Gerais"). *Fluminense* formed in these previously *caipira*-speaking areas due to the gradual influence of European migrants, causing many people to distance their speech from their original dialect and incorporate new terms.[[165]](#cite_note-165) *Fluminense* is sometimes referred to as *carioca*, however *carioca* is a more specific term referring to the accent of the [Greater Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Greater_Rio_de_Janeiro "Greater Rio de Janeiro") area by speakers with a *fluminense* dialect.
5. *[Gaúcho](/wiki/Ga%C3%BAcho_dialect "Gaúcho dialect")* – in [Rio Grande do Sul](/wiki/Rio_Grande_do_Sul "Rio Grande do Sul"), similar to *sulista*. There are many distinct accents in Rio Grande do Sul, mainly due to the heavy influx of European immigrants of diverse origins who have settled in colonies throughout the state, and to the proximity to [Spanish-speaking nations](/wiki/Hispanosphere "Hispanosphere"). The word *gaúcho* itself is a Spanish [loanword](/wiki/Loanword "Loanword") into Portuguese, of obscure [Indigenous Amerindian](/wiki/Indigenous_languages_of_the_Americas "Indigenous languages of the Americas") origins.
6. *[Mineiro](/wiki/Mineiro "Mineiro")* – [Minas Gerais](/wiki/Minas_Gerais "Minas Gerais") (but not prevalent in the [Triângulo Mineiro](/wiki/Tri%C3%A2ngulo_Mineiro "Triângulo Mineiro")). As with the *fluminense* area, its associated region was formerly a sparsely populated land where *caipira* was spoken, but [the discovery of gold and gems made it the most prosperous Brazilian region](/wiki/Minas_Gerais#History "Minas Gerais"), attracting Portuguese colonists, commoners from other parts of Brazil, and their African slaves. The south-southwestern, [southeastern](/wiki/Zona_da_Mata_(Minas_Gerais) "Zona da Mata (Minas Gerais)"), and northern areas of the state each have fairly distinctive speech, actually approximating to *caipira*, *fluminense* (popularly and often pejoratively called *carioca do brejo*, "marsh carioca"), and *baiano* respectively. [Belo Horizonte](/wiki/Belo_Horizonte "Belo Horizonte") and the area surrounding it have a distinctive accent.
7. [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Loudspeaker.svg/20px-Loudspeaker.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Loudspeaker.svg) [*Nordestino*](http://cvc.instituto-camoes.pt/hlp/geografia/som91.html)[[166]](#cite_note-ReferenceB-166) – more marked in the [Sertão](/wiki/Sert%C3%A3o "Sertão") (7), where, in the 19th and 20th centuries and especially in the area including and surrounding the *sertão* (the dry land after [Agreste](/wiki/Agreste "Agreste")) of Pernambuco and southern Ceará, it could sound less comprehensible to speakers of other Portuguese dialects than Galician or [Rioplatense Spanish](/wiki/Rioplatense_Spanish "Rioplatense Spanish"), and nowadays less distinctive from other variants in the metropolitan cities [along the coasts](/wiki/Zona_da_Mata "Zona da Mata"). It can be divided in two regional variants, one that includes the northern [Maranhão](/wiki/Maranh%C3%A3o "Maranhão") and southern of [Piauí](/wiki/Piau%C3%AD "Piauí"), and other that goes from [Ceará](/wiki/Cear%C3%A1 "Ceará") to [Alagoas](/wiki/Alagoas "Alagoas").
8. *Nortista* or *[amazofonia](/wiki/Amazofonia "Amazofonia")* – Most of [Amazon Basin](/wiki/Amazon_Basin "Amazon Basin") states, i.e. [Northern Brazil](/wiki/North_Region,_Brazil "North Region, Brazil"). Before the 20th century, most people from the *nordestino* area fleeing the droughts and their associated poverty settled here, so it has some similarities with the Portuguese dialect there spoken. The speech in and around the cities of [Belém](/wiki/Bel%C3%A9m "Belém") and [Manaus](/wiki/Manaus "Manaus") has a more European flavor in phonology, prosody and grammar.
9. *[Paulistano](/wiki/Paulistano_dialect "Paulistano dialect")* – Variants spoken around [Greater São Paulo](/wiki/Greater_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Greater São Paulo") in its maximum definition and more easterly areas of São Paulo state, as well as perhaps "educated speech" from anywhere in [the state of São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_(state) "São Paulo (state)") (where it coexists with *caipira*). *Caipira* is the hinterland sociolect of much of the [Central-Southern half](/wiki/Centro-Sul "Centro-Sul") of Brazil, nowadays conservative only in the rural areas and associated with them, that has a historically [low prestige](/wiki/Prestige_(sociolinguistics) "Prestige (sociolinguistics)") in cities as Rio de Janeiro, Curitiba, Belo Horizonte, and until some years ago, in São Paulo itself. [Sociolinguistics](/wiki/Sociolinguistics "Sociolinguistics"), or what by times is described as "[linguistic prejudice](/wiki/Linguistic_discrimination "Linguistic discrimination")", often correlated with [classism](/wiki/Class_discrimination "Class discrimination"),[[167]](#cite_note-167)[[168]](#cite_note-168)[[169]](#cite_note-169) is a polemic topic in the entirety of the country since the times of [Adoniran Barbosa](/wiki/Adoniran_Barbosa#Musical_production "Adoniran Barbosa"). Also, the "Paulistano" accent was heavily influenced by the presence of immigrants in the city of São Paulo, especially the Italians.
10. *Sertanejo* – [Center-Western states](/wiki/Center-West_Region,_Brazil "Center-West Region, Brazil"), and also much of [Tocantins](/wiki/Tocantins "Tocantins") and [Rondônia](/wiki/Rond%C3%B4nia "Rondônia"). It is closer to *mineiro*, *caipira*, *nordestino* or *nortista* depending on the location.
11. *Sulista* – The variants spoken in the areas between the northern regions of [Rio Grande do Sul](/wiki/Rio_Grande_do_Sul "Rio Grande do Sul") and southern regions of São Paulo state, encompassing most of [southern Brazil](/wiki/South_Region,_Brazil "South Region, Brazil"). The city of [Curitiba](/wiki/Curitiba "Curitiba") does have a fairly distinct accent as well, and a relative majority of speakers around and in [Florianópolis](/wiki/Florian%C3%B3polis "Florianópolis") also speak this variant (many speak *florianopolitano* or *manezinho da ilha* instead, related to the European Portuguese dialects spoken in [Azores](/wiki/Azores "Azores") and [Madeira](/wiki/Madeira "Madeira")). Speech of northern Paraná is closer to that of inland São Paulo.
12. *[Florianopolitano](/wiki/Florianopolitan_dialect "Florianopolitan dialect")* – Variants heavily influenced by European Portuguese spoken in [Florianópolis](/wiki/Florian%C3%B3polis "Florianópolis") city (due to a heavy immigration movement from Portugal, mainly its [insular regions](/wiki/Autonomous_regions_of_Portugal "Autonomous regions of Portugal")) and much of its metropolitan area, [Grande Florianópolis](/wiki/Grande_Florian%C3%B3polis "Grande Florianópolis"), said to be a continuum between those whose speech most resemble *sulista* dialects and those whose speech most resemble *fluminense* and European ones, called *manezinho da ilha*.
13. *[Carioca](/wiki/Carioca "Carioca")* – Not a dialect, but [sociolects](/wiki/Sociolect "Sociolect") of the *fluminense* variant spoken in an area roughly corresponding to [Greater Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Greater_Rio_de_Janeiro "Greater Rio de Janeiro"). It appeared after locals came in contact with the Portuguese aristocracy amidst the [Portuguese royal family fled](/wiki/Transfer_of_the_Portuguese_Court_to_Brazil "Transfer of the Portuguese Court to Brazil") in the early 19th century. There is actually a continuum between Vernacular countryside accents and the *carioca* sociolect, and the educated speech (in Portuguese *norma culta*, which most closely resembles other Brazilian Portuguese standards but with marked recent Portuguese influences, the nearest ones among the country's dialects along *florianopolitano*), so that not all people native to the state of Rio de Janeiro speak the said sociolect, but most *carioca* speakers will use the standard variant not influenced by it that is rather uniform around Brazil depending on context (emphasis or formality, for example).
14. *Brasiliense* – used in [Brasília](/wiki/Bras%C3%ADlia "Brasília") and its metropolitan area.[[170]](#cite_note-170) It is not considered a dialect, but more of a regional variant – often deemed to be closer to *fluminense* than the dialect commonly spoken in most of Goiás, *sertanejo*.
15. *Arco do desflorestamento* or *[serra amazônica](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dialeto_da_serra_amaz%C3%B4nica "pt:Dialeto da serra amazônica")* – Known in its region as the "accent of the migrants", it has similarities with *caipira*, *sertanejo* and often *sulista* that make it differing from *amazofonia* (in the opposite group of Brazilian dialects, in which it is placed along *nordestino*, *baiano*, *mineiro* and *fluminense*). It is the most recent dialect, which appeared by the settlement of families from various other Brazilian regions attracted by the cheap land offer in recently [deforested](/wiki/Deforestation "Deforestation") areas.[[171]](#cite_note-171)
16. *Recifense* – used in [Recife](/wiki/Recife "Recife") and its metropolitan area.
17. *Amazônico Ocidental* — used in the extreme [Western Amazon](/wiki/Amaz%C3%B4nia_Legal "Amazônia Legal") region, namely: Southwestern [Amazonas](/wiki/Amazonas_(Brazilian_state) "Amazonas (Brazilian state)"), including the region of [Boca do Acre](/wiki/Boca_do_Acre "Boca do Acre") and throughout the State of [Acre](/wiki/Acre_(state) "Acre (state)"), which share important historical-cultural aspects, such as, once belonging to [Peru-Bolivian Confederation](/wiki/Peru-Bolivian_Confederation "Peru-Bolivian Confederation"), the First [Amazon rubber cycle](/wiki/Amazon_rubber_cycle "Amazon rubber cycle") and [Acre Time Zone](/wiki/Time_in_Brazil "Time in Brazil"), sociologically, is considered a homogenous region. Differing from the traditional Northern dialect, in which the phonetic realization of the "s" always has the sound of *ch*, in the Brazilian Western Amazon region, there will only be the sound of *ch* whose words the "s" are in the middle of the word, as examples; *costa*, *festa* or *destino*, as well as the one observed in [dialect of the north coast](/wiki/North_coast_Portuguese "North coast Portuguese"). Within the [Brazilian countryside](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Interior_do_Brasil "pt:Interior do Brasil"), it is one of the few areas where the phonetic realization of "r" resembles those observed in the Carioca dialect (open), other examples where this phenomenon is observed: Brasília dialect and [Belo Horizonte dialect](/wiki/Mineiro "Mineiro").[[172]](#cite_note-172)

*Você*, a pronoun meaning "you", is used for educated, formal, and colloquial respectful speech in most Portuguese-speaking regions. In a few Brazilian states such as [Rio Grande do Sul](/wiki/Rio_Grande_do_Sul "Rio Grande do Sul"), Pará, among others, *você* is virtually absent from the spoken language. Riograndense and European Portuguese normally distinguishes formal from informal speech by verbal conjugation. Informal speech employs *tu* followed by second person verbs, formal language retains the formal *você*, followed by the third person conjugation.

Conjugation of verbs in *tu* has three different forms in Brazil (verb "to see": *tu viste?*, in the traditional second person; *tu viu?*, in the third person; and *tu visse?*, in the innovative second person), the conjugation used in the Brazilian states of Pará, Santa Catarina and Maranhão being generally traditional second person, the kind that is used in other Portuguese-speaking countries and learned in Brazilian schools.

The predominance of Southeastern-based media products has established *você* as the pronoun of choice for the second person singular in both writing and multimedia communications. However, in the city of Rio de Janeiro, the country's main cultural center, the usage of *tu* has been expanding ever since the end of the 20th century,[[173]](#cite_note-173) being most frequent among youngsters, and a number of studies have also shown an increase in its use in a number of other Brazilian dialects.[[174]](#cite_note-174)[[175]](#cite_note-175)

Differences between dialects are mostly of [accent](/wiki/Accent_(dialect) "Accent (dialect)") and [vocabulary](/wiki/Vocabulary "Vocabulary"), but between the Brazilian dialects and other dialects, especially in their most colloquial forms, there can also be some grammatical differences. The [Portuguese-based creoles](/wiki/Portuguese_creole "Portuguese creole") spoken in various parts of Africa, Asia, and the Americas are independent languages.

### Characterization and peculiarities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_language&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Characterization and peculiarities")]

Portuguese, like [Catalan](/wiki/Catalan_language "Catalan language"), preserves the stressed vowels of [Vulgar Latin](/wiki/Vulgar_Latin "Vulgar Latin") which became diphthongs in most other Romance languages; cf. Port., Cat., Sard. *[pedra](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/pedra "wikt:pedra")*; Fr. **[pierre](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/pierre#French "wikt:pierre")**, Sp. **[piedra](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/piedra#Spanish "wikt:piedra")**, It. **[pietra](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/pietra#Italian "wikt:pietra")**, Ro. **[piatră](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/piatr%C4%83#Romanian "wikt:piatră")**, from Lat. **[petra](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/petra#Latin "wikt:petra")** ("stone"); or Port. **[fogo](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/fogo#Portuguese "wikt:fogo")**, Cat. **[foc](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/foc#Catalan "wikt:foc")**, Sard. **[fogu](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/fogu#Sardinian "wikt:fogu")**; Sp. **[fuego](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/fuego#Spanish "wikt:fuego")**, It. **[fuoco](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/fuoco#Italian "wikt:fuoco")**, Fr. **[feu](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/feu#French "wikt:feu")**, Ro. **[foc](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/foc#Romanian "wikt:foc")**, from Lat. **[focus](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/focus#Latin "wikt:focus")** ("fire"). Another characteristic of early Portuguese was the loss of [intervocalic](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/intervocalic "wiktionary:intervocalic") *l* and *n*, sometimes followed by the merger of the two surrounding vowels, or by the insertion of an [epenthetic vowel](/wiki/Epenthesis "Epenthesis") between them: cf. Lat. **[salire](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/salire#Latin "wikt:salire")** ("to exit"), **[tenere](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/tenere#Latin "wikt:tenere")** ("to have"), **[catena](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/catena#Latin "wikt:catena")** ("jail"), Port. **[sair](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/sair#Portuguese "wikt:sair")**, **[ter](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/ter#Portuguese "wikt:ter")**, **[cadeia](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/cadeia#Portuguese "wikt:cadeia")**.

When the [elided](/wiki/Elision "Elision") consonant was *n*, it often [nasalized](/wiki/Nasalization "Nasalization") the preceding vowel: cf. Lat. **[manum](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/manum#Latin "wikt:manum")** ("hand"), **[ranam](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/ranam#Latin "wikt:ranam")** ("frog"), **[bonum](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/bonum#Latin "wikt:bonum")** ("good"), Old Portuguese **[mão](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/m%C3%A3o#Portuguese "wikt:mão")**, **[rãa](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/r%C3%A3a#Portuguese "wikt:rãa")**, **[bõo](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/b%C3%B5o#Portuguese "wikt:bõo")** (Portuguese: **[mão](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/m%C3%A3o#Portuguese "wikt:mão")**, **[rã](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/r%C3%A3#Portuguese "wikt:rã")**, **[bom](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/bom#Portuguese "wikt:bom")**). This process was the source of most of the language's distinctive nasal diphthongs. In particular, the Latin endings *-anem*, **[-anum](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/-anum#Latin "wikt:-anum")** and **[-onem](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/-onem#Latin "wikt:-onem")** became **[-ão](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/-%C3%A3o#Portuguese "wikt:-ão")** in most cases, cf. Lat. **[canis](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/canis#Latin "wikt:canis")** ("dog"), **[germanus](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/germanus#Latin "wikt:germanus")** ("brother"), **[ratio](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/ratio#Latin "wikt:ratio")** ("reason") with Modern Port. **[cão](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/c%C3%A3o#Portuguese "wikt:cão")**, **[irmão](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/irm%C3%A3o#Portuguese "wikt:irmão")**, **[razão](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/raz%C3%A3o#Portuguese "wikt:razão")**, and their plurals *-anes*, *-anos*, *-ones* normally became *-ães*, *-ãos*, *-ões*, cf. *cães*, *irmãos*, *razões*. This also occurs in the minority Swiss [Romansh](/wiki/Romansh_language "Romansh language") language in many equivalent words such as *maun* ("hand"), *bun* ("good"), or *chaun* ("dog").[[176]](#cite_note-176)

The Portuguese language is the only [Romance language](/wiki/Romance_language "Romance language") that preserves the clitic case [mesoclisis](/wiki/Mesoclisis "Mesoclisis"): cf. *dar-te-ei* (I'll give thee), *amar-te-ei* (I'll love you), *contactá-los-ei* (I'll contact them). Like [Galician](/wiki/Galician_language "Galician language"), it also retains the Latin synthetic [pluperfect](/wiki/Pluperfect "Pluperfect") tense: *eu estivera* (I had been), *eu vivera* (I had lived), *vós vivêreis* (you had lived).[[177]](#cite_note-177) [Romanian](/wiki/Romanian_language "Romanian language") also has this tense, but uses the -s- form.

## Sample text

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portuguese_language&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Sample text")]

Article 1 of the [Universal Declaration of Human Rights](/wiki/Universal_Declaration_of_Human_Rights "Universal Declaration of Human Rights") in Portuguese:[[178]](#cite_note-178)

:   *Todos os seres humanos nascem livres e iguais em dignidade e em direitos. Dotados de razão e de consciência, devem agir uns para com os outros em espírito de fraternidade.*

Article 1 of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights in English:[[179]](#cite_note-179)

:   *All human beings are born free and equal in dignity and rights. They are endowed with reason and conscience and should act towards one another in a spirit of brotherhood.*

Phonetic transcription ([Brazilian Portuguese](/wiki/Brazilian_Portuguese "Brazilian Portuguese")):

:   [ˈtoduz us ˈseɾiz uˈmɐnuz ˈnasẽȷ̃ ˈlivɾiz i‿iˈgwajz ẽȷ̃ d͡ʒigniˈdad͡ʒi‿i‿ẽȷ̃ d͡ʒiˈɾejtus | doˈtaduz d͡ʒi ʁaˈzɐ̃w i d͡ʒi kõsiˈẽsjɐ | devẽȷ̃ aˈʒiʁ ũs ˈpaɾɐ kõ‿uz ˈotɾuz ẽȷ̃‿sˈpiɾitu d͡ʒi fɾateʁniˈdad͡ʒi ‖]

Phonetic transcription ([European Portuguese](/wiki/European_Portuguese "European Portuguese")):

:   [ˈtoðuz uʃ ˈseɾɨz uˈmɐnuʒ ˈnaʃsɐ̃j ˈɫivɾɨz i‿iˈɣwajz ɐ̃j diɣniˈðaðɨ‿i‿ɐ̃j diˈɾɐjtuʃ | duˈtaðuʒ dɨ ʁɐˈzɐ̃w i dɨ kõʃsiˈẽsjɐ | ˈdevɐ̃j ɐˈʒiɾ ũʃ ˈpɐɾɐ kõ‿uz ˈotɾuz ɐ̃j‿ʃˈpiɾɨtu dɨ fɾɐtɨɾniˈðaðɨ ‖]

"A conclusão será que nos encontramos em presença de dois segmentos fonológicos /kʷ/ e /ɡʷ/, respetivamente, com uma articulação vocálica. Bisol (2005:122), tal como Freitas (1997), afirma que não estamos em presença de um ataque ramificado. Neste caso, a glide, juntamente com a vogal que a sucede, forma um ditongo no nível pós-lexical. Esta conclusão implica um aumento do número de segmentos no inventário segmental fonológico do português."
138. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-labialized_velar_-_Bisol_138-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-labialized_velar_-_Bisol_138-1) [Bisol (2005](#CITEREFBisol2005):122): "A proposta é que a sequencia consoante velar + glide posterior seja indicada no léxico como uma unidade monofonemática /kʷ/ e /ɡʷ/. O glide que, nete caso, situa-se no ataque não-ramificado, forma com a vogal seguinte um ditongo crescente em nível pós lexical. Ditongos crescentes somente se formam neste nível. Em resumo, a consoante velar e o glide posterior, quando seguidos de a/o, formam uma só unidade fonológica, ou seja, um segmento consonantal com articulação secundária vocálica, em outros termos, um segmento complexo."
